The **2016 Summer Olympics** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Jogos Olímpicos de Verão de 2016*),[[C]](#cite_note-5) officially the **Games of the XXXI Olympiad** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Jogos da XXXI Olimpíada*) and officially branded as **Rio 2016**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 5 to 21 August 2016 in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil, with preliminary events in some sports beginning on 3 August. Rio de Janeiro was announced as the host city at the [121st IOC Session](/wiki/121st_IOC_Session "121st IOC Session") in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), Denmark, on 2 October 2009.

11,238 athletes from 207 nations took part in the 2016 Games, including first-time entrants [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Kosovo at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "South Sudan at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), and the [Refugee Olympic Team](/wiki/Refugee_Olympic_Team_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Refugee Olympic Team at the 2016 Summer Olympics").[[3]](#cite_note-Olympic_Athletes-6)[[4]](#cite_note-Rio2016-7) With 306 sets of medals, the Games featured 28 [Olympic sports](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports"), including [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") and [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), which were added to the Olympic program in 2009. These sporting events took place at 33 venues in the host city and at five separate venues in the Brazilian cities of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte"), [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia"), [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília"), and [Manaus](/wiki/Manaus "Manaus").

These were the first [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") to be held in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"),[[5]](#cite_note-BBC_Sport_OC-8) as well as the first to be held in a [Portuguese-speaking country](/wiki/Portuguese-speaking_world "Portuguese-speaking world"), the first summer edition to be held entirely in the [host country's winter season](/wiki/Climate_of_Brazil "Climate of Brazil"), the first since [1968](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics") to be held in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), and the first since [2000](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics") to be held in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere").[[6]](#cite_note-9) These were also the first Summer Olympics to take place under the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) presidency of [Thomas Bach](/wiki/Thomas_Bach "Thomas Bach").[[4]](#cite_note-Rio2016-7) The games were opened by [acting president of Brazil](/wiki/President_of_Brazil "President of Brazil") (and later president), [Michel Temer](/wiki/Michel_Temer "Michel Temer") and closed by Bach.

The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "United States at the 2016 Summer Olympics") topped the [medal table](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_medal_table "2016 Summer Olympics medal table"), winning the most gold medals (46) and the highest number of medals overall (121); the U.S. team also won its 1,000th Summer Olympic gold medal. [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Great Britain at the 2016 Summer Olympics") finished second and the only country to increase its tally of medals in the Summer Olympiad immediately after being the [host nation](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Great Britain at the 2012 Summer Olympics").[[7]](#cite_note-10) [China](/wiki/China_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "China at the 2016 Summer Olympics") finished third. Host nation [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Brazil at the 2016 Summer Olympics") won seven gold medals and 19 total medals, its best result at any Olympics, finishing in thirteenth place. [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Bahrain at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Fiji at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Ivory Coast at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Jordan at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Kosovo at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Puerto Rico at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Singapore at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Tajikistan at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Vietnam at the 2016 Summer Olympics") all won their first gold medals, as did the group of [Independent Olympic Athletes](/wiki/Independent_Olympic_Athletes_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Independent Olympic Athletes at the 2016 Summer Olympics") (from [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait")).

## Bidding process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Bidding process")]

Main article: [Bids for the 2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Bids for the 2016 Summer Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Cidade_Candidata_%28Rio_de_Janeiro_for_the_2016_Olympic_Games%29.JPG/250px-Cidade_Candidata_%28Rio_de_Janeiro_for_the_2016_Olympic_Games%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cidade_Candidata_(Rio_de_Janeiro_for_the_2016_Olympic_Games).JPG)

A young girl adding her signature in support of Rio de Janeiro's candidacy

[![Several people dressed in formal attire sit behind a bench. Behind them, there are repeated graphics representing Rio 2016.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Rio_de_Janeiro_2016_press_conference.jpg/250px-Rio_de_Janeiro_2016_press_conference.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_de_Janeiro_2016_press_conference.jpg)

The bid committee, led by [Carlos Arthur Nuzman](/wiki/Carlos_Arthur_Nuzman "Carlos Arthur Nuzman"), giving a press conference.

The bidding process for the 2016 Summer Olympics was officially launched on 16 May 2007.[[8]](#cite_note-11) The first step for each city was to submit an initial application to the International Olympic Committee by 13 September 2007, confirming their intention to bid. Completed official bid files containing answers to a 25-question IOC form were to be submitted by each city by the deadline of 14 January 2008. On 4 June 2008, two months before the [Beijing Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), four candidate cities were chosen for the shortlist: [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), and [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), which had already hosted the Summer Olympics in [1964](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics"). Three cities—[Baku](/wiki/Baku "Baku"), [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha"), and [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague")—failed to reach the candidature phase.

[Nawal El Moutawakel](/wiki/Nawal_El_Moutawakel "Nawal El Moutawakel") of Morocco headed the 10-member Evaluation Commission, having also chaired the evaluation commission for the [2012 Summer Olympics bids](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics_bids "2012 Summer Olympics bids"), which was awarded to [London](/wiki/London "London"), United Kingdom. The commission made on-site inspections in the second quarter of 2009. They issued a comprehensive technical appraisal for IOC members on 2 September, one month before the elections.[[9]](#cite_note-12)

Many safeguards were put in place to prevent bidding cities from communicating with or directly influencing the 115 IOC members eligible to vote in the elections. For example, cities could not invite any IOC member to visit, nor could they send anything that could be construed as a gift. Nonetheless, bidding cities invested large sums in their PR and media programs to indirectly influence the IOC members by garnering domestic support and backing from sports media and general international media.

> Ultimately, you are communicating with just 115 people and each one has influencers and pressure groups but you are still speaking to no more than about 1,500 people, perhaps 5,000 in the broadest sense. It is not just about getting ads out there but it is about a targeted and very carefully planned campaign.

— Jon Tibbs, a consultant on the [Tokyo bid](/wiki/Tokyo_bid_for_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Tokyo bid for the 2016 Summer Olympics")[[10]](#cite_note-13)

The final voting was held in Copenhagen on 2 October 2009, with Madrid and Rio de Janeiro considered favorites to secure the Games. Chicago was eliminated after the first round of voting, and Tokyo after the second (The latter city would eventually be awarded the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in 2013). Rio de Janeiro took a significant lead over Madrid, heading into the final round; the lead was held, and Rio de Janeiro was announced as host of the 2016 Summer Olympics.

## Development and preparations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development and preparations")]

On 26 June 2011, it was reported on [Around The Rings](/wiki/Around_The_Rings "Around The Rings") that Roderlei Generali, the COO of the Rio de Janeiro Organizing Committee for the Olympic Games, resigned just one year after taking the job at ROOC. This came just five months after CCO Flávio Pestana quit for personal reasons.[[12]](#cite_note-15) Pestana withdrew later during the [2012 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics "2012 Summer Paralympics"), and Renato Ciuchin was then appointed as COO.[[13]](#cite_note-16)

### Venues and infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Venues and infrastructure")]

Main article: [Venues of the 2016 Summer Olympics and Paralympics](/wiki/Venues_of_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_and_Paralympics "Venues of the 2016 Summer Olympics and Paralympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Rio_de_Janeiro_bid_venues_for_the_2016_Summer_Olympics.svg/330px-Rio_de_Janeiro_bid_venues_for_the_2016_Summer_Olympics.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rio_de_Janeiro_bid_venues_for_the_2016_Summer_Olympics.svg)

Venues of the 2016 Summer Olympics

Events took place at eighteen existing venues, nine new venues constructed specifically for the Games, and seven temporary venues.[[14]](#cite_note-CF-17)

For the events held in Rio de Janeiro, each event was held in one of four geographically segregated Olympic clusters–[Barra](/wiki/Barra_da_Tijuca "Barra da Tijuca"), [Copacabana](/wiki/Copacabana_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Copacabana (Rio de Janeiro)"), [Deodoro](/wiki/Deodoro,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Deodoro, Rio de Janeiro"), and [Maracanã](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_(neighborhood) "Maracanã (neighborhood)")–as was done for the [2007 Pan American Games](/wiki/2007_Pan_American_Games "2007 Pan American Games").[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19) Several of the venues were located at the Barra Cluster Olympic Park.[[14]](#cite_note-CF-17) Nearly half of the athletes could reach their venues in less than 10 minutes, and almost 75 per cent could do so in less than 25 minutes. Of the 34 competition venues, eight underwent some permanent works, seven were totally temporary and nine were constructed as permanent legacy venues.[[1]](#cite_note-olympic.org/rio-2016-2)

The largest venue at the Games in terms of [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") was the 75,000-seat [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium"), which served as the ceremonies venue and site of the football finals.[[14]](#cite_note-CF-17) The second largest stadium was the 60,000-seat [Estádio Olímpico Nilton Santos](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Nilton_Santos "Estádio Olímpico Nilton Santos") (formerly João Havelange during the Games), which hosted track and field events.[[14]](#cite_note-CF-17) The athletes' village was said to be the largest in Olympic history. Fittings included about 80,000 chairs, 70,000 tables, 29,000 mattresses, 60,000 clothes hangers, 6,000 television sets and 10,000 smartphones.[[17]](#cite_note-rio2016.com-20)

#### Olympic Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Olympic Park")]

Main article: [Barra Olympic Park](/wiki/Barra_Olympic_Park "Barra Olympic Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Parque_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Rio_2016_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Parque_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Rio_2016_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parque_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Rio_2016_(cropped).jpg)

[Barra Olympic Park](/wiki/Barra_Olympic_Park "Barra Olympic Park")

The Barra Olympic Park is a cluster of nine sporting venues in [Barra da Tijuca](/wiki/Barra_da_Tijuca "Barra da Tijuca"), in the west zone of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. The site was formerly occupied by the [Autódromo Internacional Nelson Piquet](/wiki/Aut%C3%B3dromo_Internacional_Nelson_Piquet "Autódromo Internacional Nelson Piquet"), also known as the Jacarepaguá Formula One circuit.[[18]](#cite_note-abcnews-2013-21)

The nine venues within the Olympic Park were:[[19]](#cite_note-22)[[20]](#cite_note-federal-venues-23)

* [Carioca Arena 1](/wiki/Carioca_Arena_1 "Carioca Arena 1") – basketball (capacity: 16,000)
* [Carioca Arena 2](/wiki/Carioca_Arena_2 "Carioca Arena 2") – wrestling, judo (capacity: 10,000)
* [Carioca Arena 3](/wiki/Carioca_Arena_3 "Carioca Arena 3") – fencing, taekwondo (capacity: 10,000)
* [Future Arena](/wiki/Future_Arena "Future Arena") – handball (capacity: 12,000)
* [Maria Lenk Aquatics Centre](/wiki/Maria_Lenk_Aquatics_Centre "Maria Lenk Aquatics Centre") – diving, synchronized swimming, water polo (capacity: 5,000)
* [Olympic Aquatics Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Aquatics_Stadium "Olympic Aquatics Stadium") – swimming, water polo play-offs (capacity: 15,000)
* [Olympic Tennis Centre](/wiki/Olympic_Tennis_Centre_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Olympic Tennis Centre (Rio de Janeiro)") – tennis (capacity: 10,000 Main Court)
* [Rio Olympic Arena](/wiki/Rio_Olympic_Arena "Rio Olympic Arena") – gymnastics (capacity: 12,000)
* [Rio Olympic Velodrome](/wiki/Rio_Olympic_Velodrome "Rio Olympic Velodrome") – track cycling (capacity: 5,000)

#### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Football")]

### Urban renovations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Urban renovations")]

Main article: [Port of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Port_of_Rio_de_Janeiro "Port of Rio de Janeiro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/VLT_Rio_09_2016_3355.jpg/250px-VLT_Rio_09_2016_3355.jpg)](/wiki/File:VLT_Rio_09_2016_3355.jpg)

Mauá Square, with the [Museum of Tomorrow](/wiki/Museum_of_Tomorrow "Museum of Tomorrow"), designed by [Santiago Calatrava](/wiki/Santiago_Calatrava "Santiago Calatrava"), and the [light rail](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_Light_Rail "Rio de Janeiro Light Rail")

Rio's historical [downtown](/wiki/Centro_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Centro (Rio de Janeiro)") underwent an urban waterfront revitalization project known as *[Porto Maravilha](/wiki/Porto_Maravilha "Porto Maravilha")*, covering 5 km2 (1.9 mi2) in area. The project aimed to redevelop the port area, increase the city center's attractiveness, and enhance Rio's competitive position in the global economy.[[21]](#cite_note-24)

The urban renovation involved 700 km (430 mi) of public networks for water supply, sanitation, drainage, electricity, gas and telecom; 4 km (2.5 mi) of tunnels; 70 km (43 mi) of roads; 650 km2 (250 mi2) of sidewalks; 17 km (11 mi) of bike path; 15,000 trees; and three sanitation treatment plants. As part of this renovation, a new tram was built from the [Santos Dumont Airport](/wiki/Santos_Dumont_Airport "Santos Dumont Airport") to Rodoviária Novo Rio, due to open in April 2016.[[22]](#cite_note-25)

The Games required over 200 kilometers of security fencing. A 15,000 square meter warehouse in Barra da Tijuca was used to assemble and supply the furniture and fittings for the Olympic Village. The second warehouse of 90,000 square meters in Duque de Caxias, near the roads that provide access to the venues, contained all the equipment needed for the sporting events.[[17]](#cite_note-rio2016.com-20)

### Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Medals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Medalhas_Rio_2016_%282%29.jpg/330px-Medalhas_Rio_2016_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Medalhas_Rio_2016_(2).jpg)

Medals of the 2016 Summer Olympics

The medals were produced by the [Casa da Moeda do Brasil](/wiki/Casa_da_Moeda_do_Brasil "Casa da Moeda do Brasil") (the Brazilian National Mint). The medal design was unveiled on 15 June 2016. They were designed to be environmentally friendly using recycled materials; the bronze and silver medals contained 30% recycled materials. The gold medals were produced using gold that had been mined and extracted according to a set of sustainability criteria, such as being extracted without the use of mercury. The medals feature a wreath design on the front, and in keeping with tradition, the obverse features [Nike](/wiki/Nike_(mythology) "Nike (mythology)"), the Greek goddess of victory. A wooden carrying box accompanied each medal. Medalists were also awarded a trophy in the shape of the Games' emblem.[[23]](#cite_note-ioc-medaldesign-26)[[24]](#cite_note-yahoo-noflowers-27)

In May 2017, an [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") article disclosed that over 100 athletes who had won medals at the Rio Olympics reported that their medals were showing some damage, including black spots, flaking, or surface degrading. Rio officials offered to replace any defective medals and found problems with 6 to 7 percent of all those awarded.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

### Torch relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Torch relay")]

Main article: [2016 Summer Olympics torch relay](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_torch_relay "2016 Summer Olympics torch relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Passagem_da_Tocha_Ol%C3%ADmpica_em_SP_%2828605511640%29.jpg/250px-Passagem_da_Tocha_Ol%C3%ADmpica_em_SP_%2828605511640%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Passagem_da_Tocha_Ol%C3%ADmpica_em_SP_(28605511640).jpg)

Basketball player [Anderson Varejão](/wiki/Anderson_Varej%C3%A3o "Anderson Varejão") carrying the torch in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Rio_2016_-_Volunt%C3%A1rios_organizando_barreiras_%2829066118645%29.jpg/250px-Rio_2016_-_Volunt%C3%A1rios_organizando_barreiras_%2829066118645%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_2016_-_Volunt%C3%A1rios_organizando_barreiras_(29066118645).jpg)

Volunteers working at the Olympic Stadium during the Games

The Olympic flame was lit on 21 April 2016 at the [Temple of Hera](/wiki/Temple_of_Hera,_Olympia "Temple of Hera, Olympia") in [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece"), the traditional start of the Greek phase of the torch relay. The flame was handed over to the Brazilian organisers in a ceremony at the [Panathenaic Stadium](/wiki/Panathenaic_Stadium "Panathenaic Stadium") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") on 27 April. A brief stop-off was made in Switzerland to visit the IOC headquarters and the [Olympic Museum](/wiki/Olympic_Museum "Olympic Museum") in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"), as well as the [United Nations Office at Geneva](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_at_Geneva "United Nations Office at Geneva").[[26]](#cite_note-starts-29)

The torch relay began its journey around Brazil on 3 May at the capital [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília"). The flame visited more than 300 Brazilian cities, including all 26 state capitals and the [Brazilian Federal District](/wiki/Brazilian_Federal_District "Brazilian Federal District").[[27]](#cite_note-dressage-30) The relay ended in Rio de Janeiro on 5 August when the flame was used to light the Olympic cauldron during the [opening ceremony](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony "2016 Summer Olympics opening ceremony").

### Volunteers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Volunteers")]

Unpaid volunteers performed a variety of tasks before and during the Games. A target of 50,000 volunteers was set as early as 2012. More than 240,000 applications were received when recruitment took place in 2014. The clothing worn by the volunteers included yellow polo shirts and jackets, beige trousers, white socks, and green trainers, which they collected from the Uniform Distribution and Accreditation Centre. Volunteers also wore photo accreditation badges which were allocated to officials, athletes, family members, and media, allowing them to gain access to specific venues and buildings around the site.[[28]](#cite_note-31) Many volunteers gave up their roles due to long working hours and insufficient free meals.[[29]](#cite_note-32)

### Ticketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ticketing")]

The ticket prices were announced on 16 September 2014, all of which were sold in [Brazilian reais](/wiki/Brazilian_reais "Brazilian reais") (BRL). A total of 7.5 million tickets were to be sold in total, with ticket prices ranging from BRL 40 for many events to BRL 4,600 for the most expensive seats at the opening ceremony. About 3.8 million of these tickets were available for BRL 70 or less.[[30]](#cite_note-33)[[31]](#cite_note-34)

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Arena_do_Futuro_Rio_2016.jpg/250px-Arena_do_Futuro_Rio_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arena_do_Futuro_Rio_2016.jpg)

[Future Arena](/wiki/Future_Arena "Future Arena"), a temporary venue designed for future reconstruction into school buildings

As an aspect of its bid, Rio's organizing committee planned to focus on [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") and [environmental protection](/wiki/Environmental_protection "Environmental protection") as a theme of the 2016 Games, going on to dub them a "Green Games for a Blue Planet".[[32]](#cite_note-atlantic-greengames-35) As legacy projects, organizers intended to introduce a wider array of public transport options, upgrade the infrastructure of the [favelas](/wiki/Favela "Favela") to provide improved transport and access to utilities, upgrade Rio's sewer system to remediate the level of pollution in the [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay"),[[32]](#cite_note-atlantic-greengames-35)[[33]](#cite_note-guardian-fundingclean-36) and plant 24 million seedlings to offset the expected carbon emissions of the Games. However, some of these projects met with delays or faced economic shortfalls, leading some critics to believe that Rio would not be able to accomplish them.[[32]](#cite_note-atlantic-greengames-35)[[34]](#cite_note-vicenews-enviro-37)

The focus on environmental protection also influenced the implementation of certain Olympic protocols. To reduce emissions, the Olympic cauldron was designed to be smaller than previous iterations, using a [kinetic sculpture](/wiki/Kinetic_sculpture "Kinetic sculpture") to enhance its appearance in place of a larger body of flames.[[35]](#cite_note-dezeen-cauldron-38) The bronze and silver medals, as well as the ribbons on all medals, were designed to incorporate recycled materials.[[23]](#cite_note-ioc-medaldesign-26)[[24]](#cite_note-yahoo-noflowers-27) The athletes were not presented with flowers during the medal ceremonies, as had been the tradition at previous Olympics (although floral displays were still used as part of the staging of medal presentations). The organizers considered the practice to be wasteful because the flowers were often thrown away and "would struggle to survive in the tropical Brazilian climate" if kept. The podiums were designed using materials that could be recycled to make furniture.[[24]](#cite_note-yahoo-noflowers-27)[[36]](#cite_note-mashable-noflowers-39)

The [Future Arena](/wiki/Future_Arena "Future Arena"), the venue for the handball competitions, was designed as a temporary modular structure whose components could be reconstructed after the Games to build schools.[[37]](#cite_note-dezeen-futurearena-40) However, as of November 2017, the arena was still standing due to lack of funds to dismantle it and no allocation of funds to do so in the 2018 budget.[[38]](#cite_note-41) Portions of the opening ceremony were dedicated to the issue of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[39]](#cite_note-huff-climatechange-42)

## The Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The Games")]

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main articles: [2016 Summer Olympics opening ceremony](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony "2016 Summer Olympics opening ceremony") and [2016 Summer Olympics Parade of Nations](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_Parade_of_Nations "2016 Summer Olympics Parade of Nations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/2016_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony_1035301-05082016-_v9a2048_04.08.16.jpg/250px-2016_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony_1035301-05082016-_v9a2048_04.08.16.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony_1035301-05082016-_v9a2048_04.08.16.jpg)

A scene from the [opening ceremony](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_opening_ceremony "2016 Summer Olympics opening ceremony")

The opening ceremony took place at [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium") on 5 August 2016, directed by [Fernando Meirelles](/wiki/Fernando_Meirelles "Fernando Meirelles"), [Daniela Thomas](/wiki/Daniela_Thomas "Daniela Thomas"), and [Andrucha Waddington](/wiki/Andrucha_Waddington "Andrucha Waddington").[[40]](#cite_note-Guardian_2016-08-06-43) The ceremony highlighted aspects of Brazilian history and culture, and featured a segment narrated by [Fernanda Montenegro](/wiki/Fernanda_Montenegro "Fernanda Montenegro") and [Judi Dench](/wiki/Judi_Dench "Judi Dench") with an appeal to [environmental conservation](/wiki/Nature_conservation "Nature conservation") and the prevention of [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming").[[39]](#cite_note-huff-climatechange-42)[[41]](#cite_note-torstar-ochighlights-44) The crowd in the stadium numbered 60,000 and the event was broadcast to an estimated global audience of three billion.[[5]](#cite_note-BBC_Sport_OC-8)

The ceremony included the inaugural presentation of the [Olympic Laurel](/wiki/Olympic_Laurel "Olympic Laurel"), an honor bestowed by the IOC on those that have made "significant achievements in education, culture, development and peace through sport"; the trophy was awarded to Kenyan athlete [Kipchoge Keino](/wiki/Kipchoge_Keino "Kipchoge Keino").[[42]](#cite_note-45) The Games were officially opened by the [acting president](/wiki/Acting_president "Acting president") of Brazil, [Michel Temer](/wiki/Michel_Temer "Michel Temer").[[43]](#cite_note-46)

The Olympic cauldron was lit by long-distance runner [Vanderlei Cordeiro de Lima](/wiki/Vanderlei_Cordeiro_de_Lima "Vanderlei Cordeiro de Lima"),[[5]](#cite_note-BBC_Sport_OC-8) the [men's marathon](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_marathon "Athletics at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Men's marathon") bronze medalist at the [2004 Olympics](/wiki/2004_Olympics "2004 Olympics"), who had also received the IOC's [Pierre de Coubertin medal](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin_medal "Pierre de Coubertin medal") for sportsmanship after [being attacked by a spectator](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_marathon#Incident "Athletics at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Men's marathon") and losing his lead in the race.[[40]](#cite_note-Guardian_2016-08-06-43)[[44]](#cite_note-47) The cauldron was originally expected to be lit by Brazilian footballer [Pelé](/wiki/Pel%C3%A9 "Pelé"), but he declined to participate due to health problems.[[41]](#cite_note-torstar-ochighlights-44)[[45]](#cite_note-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49)

Following the opening ceremony, a public cauldron was lit in front of the [Candelária Church](/wiki/Candel%C3%A1ria_Church "Candelária Church") by Jorge Gomes, a 14-year-old Brazilian athlete who had escaped from poverty to train as a runner.[[47]](#cite_note-cbcnews-outdoorcauldron-50)[[48]](#cite_note-jorge-51)

#### New sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: New sports")]

See also: [121st IOC Session](/wiki/121st_IOC_Session "121st IOC Session")

In April 2008, the IOC began accepting applications for two new sports to be introduced to the Olympic programme. Baseball and [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") (which were both dropped in 2005), [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [roller sports](/wiki/Roller_sports "Roller sports"), and [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") all applied to be included on the programme. Formal presentations were made to the IOC executive board in June 2009.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

In August, the executive board initially gave its approval to [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens")—a seven-player version of rugby union—by a majority vote; baseball/softball, roller sports, and squash were removed from contention, leaving golf, karate, and rugby sevens in the running. A final vote was held on 9 October 2009, the closing day of the 121st IOC Session. At this session, a new voting system was in place: a sport now needed only a simple majority from the full IOC committee for approval rather than the two-thirds majority previously required.[[50]](#cite_note-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54)

The 121st IOC Session decided to add rugby sevens and golf to the Rio 2016 Olympic programme.[[52]](#cite_note-55) The tally for rugby was 81 in favor, with eight against,[[53]](#cite_note-56) and golf was approved by 63 votes to 26.[[54]](#cite_note-57) Neither of these two sports was new to the Summer Olympics; rugby last featured in [1924](/wiki/Rugby_union_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Rugby union at the 1924 Summer Olympics"), and golf in [1904](/wiki/Golf_at_the_1904_Summer_Olympics "Golf at the 1904 Summer Olympics").

In May 2012, the [International Sailing Federation](/wiki/International_Sailing_Federation "International Sailing Federation") announced that [windsurfing](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing") would be replaced by [kitesurfing](/wiki/Kitesurfing "Kitesurfing") at the 2016 Olympics,[[55]](#cite_note-58) but this decision was reversed in November.[[56]](#cite_note-59)

### Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Records")]

Main article: [World and Olympic records set at the 2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/World_and_Olympic_records_set_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "World and Olympic records set at the 2016 Summer Olympics")

Twenty-seven world records and ninety-one Olympic records were set during the 2016 Summer Olympics. The records were set in archery, athletics, canoeing, cycling track, modern pentathlon, rowing, shooting, swimming, and weightlifting.

### Event scheduling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Event scheduling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Candel%C3%A1ria_cauldron_at_2016_Summer_Olympics.jpg/250px-Candel%C3%A1ria_cauldron_at_2016_Summer_Olympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:Candel%C3%A1ria_cauldron_at_2016_Summer_Olympics.jpg)

The public cauldron, located outside the [Candelária Church](/wiki/Candel%C3%A1ria_Church "Candelária Church")

A number of events, most notably in aquatics, beach volleyball and track and field, were scheduled with sessions and matches occurring as late as 10:00 p.m. to midnight BRT. These scheduling practices were influenced primarily by United States broadcast rightsholder [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), whose substantial rights fees are one of the major sources of revenue for the IOC, who therefore allowed NBC to have influence on event scheduling to maximize U.S. television ratings when possible (on 7 May 2014, NBC agreed to a US$7.75 billion contract extension to air the Olympics through 2032, including US$1.23 billion for Rio 2016),[[57]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-61) as well as the main Brazilian rightsholder [Rede Globo](/wiki/Rede_Globo "Rede Globo"). As Brasília time is only one hour ahead of the U.S. [Eastern Time Zone](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"), certain marquee events were scheduled to occur during U.S. [primetime](/wiki/Primetime "Primetime") hours (traditionally 8:00 to 11:00 p.m. ET, 9:00 p.m. to midnight BRT), allowing them to be broadcast live on the east coast as opposed to being [delayed](/wiki/Broadcast_delay "Broadcast delay"). This practice was also beneficial to Globo; a Brazilian critic noted that the network very rarely preempts its primetime [telenovelas](/wiki/Telenovela "Telenovela"), as they are among the highest-rated programs in the country.[[59]](#cite_note-cbc-midnightmadness-62)[[60]](#cite_note-couriermail-latenight-63)[[61]](#cite_note-nyt-globonovelas-64)[[62]](#cite_note-65)

### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main articles: [2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony "2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony") and [2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony flag bearers](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony_flag_bearers "2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony flag bearers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Terminam_os_Jogos_Ol%C3%ADmpicos_Rio_2016_%2829040726262%29.jpg/250px-Terminam_os_Jogos_Ol%C3%ADmpicos_Rio_2016_%2829040726262%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terminam_os_Jogos_Ol%C3%ADmpicos_Rio_2016_(29040726262).jpg)

2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony at [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium")

The [closing ceremony](/wiki/Olympic_Games_ceremony#Closing "Olympic Games ceremony") of the 2016 Summer Olympics was held on 21 August 2016 from 20:00 to 22:50 BRT at the [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium").[[63]](#cite_note-66) As per traditional Olympic protocol, the ceremony featured cultural presentations from both the current (Brazil) and following (Japan) host countries, as well as closing remarks by IOC president [Thomas Bach](/wiki/Thomas_Bach "Thomas Bach"), who declared the Games closed, and the [Games' organizing committee](/wiki/Rio_2016_Organising_Committee_for_the_Olympic_and_Paralympic_Games "Rio 2016 Organising Committee for the Olympic and Paralympic Games") leader [Carlos Arthur Nuzman](/wiki/Carlos_Arthur_Nuzman "Carlos Arthur Nuzman"), the official handover of the [Olympic flag](/wiki/Olympic_symbols#Flag "Olympic symbols") from Rio de Janeiro mayor [Eduardo Paes](/wiki/Eduardo_Paes "Eduardo Paes") to [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") governor [Yuriko Koike](/wiki/Yuriko_Koike "Yuriko Koike"), whose city will host the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics"), and the extinguishing of the Olympic flame.[[64]](#cite_note-bbc-67)

The creative director for the ceremony was [Rosa Magalhães](/wiki/Rosa_Magalh%C3%A3es "Rosa Magalhães").[[65]](#cite_note-68) Amid heavy rainfall, the ceremony began with interpretive dancers representing various landmarks in the host city. [Martinho da Vila](/wiki/Martinho_da_Vila "Martinho da Vila") then performed a rendition of "[Carinhoso](/wiki/Carinhoso_(song) "Carinhoso (song)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carinhoso "pt:Carinhoso")]" by [Pixinguinha](/wiki/Pixinguinha "Pixinguinha"). In another segment, introducing the athletes, singer [Roberta Sá](/wiki/Roberta_S%C3%A1 "Roberta Sá") channeled [Carmen Miranda](/wiki/Carmen_Miranda "Carmen Miranda"), the fruit-headdress-wearing, mid-century Hollywood diva who endures as a beloved [camp figure](/wiki/Camp_(style) "Camp (style)"). The Parade of Flags followed shortly after a choir of 27 children, representing the [states of Brazil](/wiki/States_of_Brazil "States of Brazil"), sang the [Brazilian national anthem](/wiki/Brazilian_national_anthem "Brazilian national anthem").

### Cost

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Cost")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Centro_Ol%C3%ADmpico_de_Deodoro.jpg/250px-Centro_Ol%C3%ADmpico_de_Deodoro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Centro_Ol%C3%ADmpico_de_Deodoro.jpg)

[Deodoro Olympic Whitewater Stadium](/wiki/Deodoro_Olympic_Whitewater_Stadium "Deodoro Olympic Whitewater Stadium")

The Oxford Olympics Study 2016 estimated the out-turn cost of the Rio 2016 Summer Olympics at US$4.6 billion in 2015-dollars. This figure included sports-related costs, that is, (i) *operational costs* incurred by the organizing committee to stage the Games, of which the largest components were technology, transportation, workforce, and administration costs, while other operational costs included security, catering, ceremonies, and medical services, and (ii) *direct capital costs* incurred by the host city and country or private investors to build the competition venues, the Olympic village, international broadcast center, and media and press center, which were required to host the Games.[[66]](#cite_note-cost-69)

Indirect capital costs were not included, such as for road, rail, or airport infrastructure, for hotel upgrades, or other business investment incurred in preparation for the Games but not directly related to staging the Games. The Rio Olympics' cost of US$4.6 billion compares with costs of US$40–44 billion for [Beijing 2008](/wiki/Beijing_2008 "Beijing 2008") and US$51 billion for [Sochi 2014](/wiki/Sochi_2014 "Sochi 2014"), the two most expensive Olympics in history. The average cost of the Summer Games since 1960 is US$5.2 billion.[[66]](#cite_note-cost-69)

## Participating National Olympic Committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Participating National Olympic Committees")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Rio2016_julho_VilaAtletas_Barra_002_2659_-c-2016_GabrielHeusi_HeusiAction.jpg/250px-Rio2016_julho_VilaAtletas_Barra_002_2659_-c-2016_GabrielHeusi_HeusiAction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio2016_julho_VilaAtletas_Barra_002_2659_-c-2016_GabrielHeusi_HeusiAction.jpg)

[Rio 2016 Olympic Village](/wiki/Rio_2016_Olympic_Village "Rio 2016 Olympic Village")

All 205 [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee") qualified at least one athlete.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first three nations to qualify athletes for the Games were Germany, Great Britain, and the Netherlands, who each qualified four athletes for the team dressage by winning medals in the team event at the [2014 FEI World Equestrian Games](/wiki/2014_FEI_World_Equestrian_Games "2014 FEI World Equestrian Games").[[67]](#cite_note-70)

As host nation, Brazil received automatic entry for some sports including in all cycling disciplines and six places for weightlifting events.[[68]](#cite_note-cyclingquotas-71)[[69]](#cite_note-brazilweightlifting-72)

The 2016 Summer Olympics were the first Games in which [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Kosovo at the 2016 Summer Olympics") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "South Sudan at the 2016 Summer Olympics") were eligible to participate. Bulgarian and Russian weightlifters were banned from Rio Olympics for numerous anti-doping violations.[[70]](#cite_note-73)[[71]](#cite_note-74)

[Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") was banned in October 2015 for the second time in five years over government interference in the country's Olympic committee. Kuwaiti athletes instead participated as "Independent Olympic Athletes".[[72]](#cite_note-75)

### Refugee athletes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Refugee athletes")]

Main article: [Refugee Olympic Team at the 2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Refugee_Olympic_Team_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Refugee Olympic Team at the 2016 Summer Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Rio_2016_Refugees_1033413-29072016-_dsc1069.jpg/250px-Rio_2016_Refugees_1033413-29072016-_dsc1069.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_2016_Refugees_1033413-29072016-_dsc1069.jpg)

Refugee Olympic team arriving in Rio de Janeiro

Due to the [European migrant crisis](/wiki/European_migrant_crisis "European migrant crisis") and other reasons, the IOC allowed athletes to compete as [Independent Olympians](/wiki/Independent_Olympians_at_the_Olympic_Games "Independent Olympians at the Olympic Games") under the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag"). During [the previous Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), refugees were ineligible to compete because of their inability to represent their home NOCs.[[73]](#cite_note-:0-76) On 2 March 2016, the IOC finalized plans for a specific [Refugee Olympic Team (ROT)](/wiki/Refugee_Olympic_Athletes_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Refugee Olympic Athletes at the 2016 Summer Olympics"); out of 43 refugee athletes deemed potentially eligible, 10 were chosen to form the team.[[74]](#cite_note-BBC-ROA-77)

### Independent athletes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Independent athletes")]

Main article: [Independent Olympic Athletes at the 2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Independent_Olympic_Athletes_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Independent Olympic Athletes at the 2016 Summer Olympics")

Due to the suspension of the National Olympic Committee of Kuwait, participants from Kuwait were allowed to participate under the Olympic Flag as Independent Olympic Athletes.

In November 2015, [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Russia at the 2016 Summer Olympics") was provisionally suspended from all international track and field athletic competitions by the [International Association of Athletics Federations](/wiki/International_Association_of_Athletics_Federations "International Association of Athletics Federations") (IAAF) following a [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (WADA) report into a [doping program](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia") in the country.[[75]](#cite_note-bbc.com-78) The IAAF announced that it would allow individual Russian athletes to apply for "exceptional eligibility" to participate in the Games as "neutral" athletes if it was independently verified that they had not engaged in doping nor in the Russian doping program.[[76]](#cite_note-guardian-yuliya2-79)

On 24 July 2016, the IOC rejected the IAAF and WADA's recommendations to allow athletes to compete neutrally, stating that the [Olympic Charter](/wiki/Olympic_Charter "Olympic Charter") "does not foresee such 'neutral athletes'" and that it was each country's National Olympic Committee decision on which athletes would be competing.[[77]](#cite_note-:322-80) As a result, Russian athletes competed under the Russian flag, although they would compete under a neutral flag in the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") following several developments concerning the doping investigation.

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

Main article: [List of 2016 Summer Olympics broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_2016_Summer_Olympics_broadcasters "List of 2016 Summer Olympics broadcasters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Rio2016_maio_ParqueOlimpico_Barra_10.jpg/250px-Rio2016_maio_ParqueOlimpico_Barra_10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio2016_maio_ParqueOlimpico_Barra_10.jpg)

International Broadcast Centre (right) at [Barra Olympic Park](/wiki/Barra_Olympic_Park "Barra Olympic Park")

[Olympic Broadcasting Services](/wiki/Olympic_Broadcasting_Services "Olympic Broadcasting Services") served as the host broadcaster for the 2016 Games. Produced from a total of seven mobile units, OBS distributed 40,000 hours of television footage and 60,000 hours of digital footage of the Games to its international rightsholders. For the first time in Olympic history, digital-oriented footage exceeded the amount of television-oriented footage. The International Broadcast Centre was constructed in the Barra da Tijuca cluster.[[85]](#cite_note-tvt-broadcasting-90) [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") and OBS once again filmed portions of the Games, including the opening ceremony and selected events, in [8K resolution](/wiki/8K_resolution "8K resolution") video. Additionally, expanding upon a 180-degree trial at the [2016 Winter Youth Olympics](/wiki/2016_Winter_Youth_Olympics "2016 Winter Youth Olympics"), 85 hours of video content were originated in [360-degree](/wiki/360-degree_video "360-degree video") [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") formats.[[86]](#cite_note-variety-olympicsvr-91) In the United States, [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") offered [4K](/wiki/4K_resolution "4K resolution") content downconverted from the 8K footage and with [HDR](/wiki/HDR_video "HDR video") and [Dolby Atmos](/wiki/Dolby_Atmos "Dolby Atmos") support, to participating television providers.[[87]](#cite_note-thr-4kolympics-92) Owing to their expertise in domestic broadcasts of the new sports introduced in Rio, [NBCU](/wiki/Golf_Channel_on_NBC "Golf Channel on NBC") and [Sky New Zealand](/wiki/Sky_New_Zealand "Sky New Zealand") staff handled the production of the golf and rugby sevens events on behalf of OBS.[[85]](#cite_note-tvt-broadcasting-90)

In August 2009, the IOC reached a deal to sell domestic broadcast rights for the 2016 Summer Olympics to [Grupo Globo](/wiki/Grupo_Globo "Grupo Globo"). Replacing [Record](/wiki/Grupo_Record "Grupo Record"), the deal covers free-to-air coverage on [Rede Globo](/wiki/Rede_Globo "Rede Globo"), [pay TV](/wiki/Globosat "Globosat"), and digital rights to the Games. In turn, Globo sublicensed partial free-to-air rights to [Record](/wiki/Record_(TV_network) "Record (TV network)"), along with [Band](/wiki/Rede_Bandeirantes "Rede Bandeirantes"). IOC board member [Richard Carrión](/wiki/Richard_Carri%C3%B3n "Richard Carrión") described the agreement as "unprecedented", touting that "by working with Brazil's leading media organizations, we are confident that this represents a great deal for Olympic fans in the region. There will be a huge increase in the amount of Olympic action broadcast, both during and outside Games time, and Brazilians will have more choice of how, when and where they follow their Olympic Games."[[88]](#cite_note-93)

### Olympic Golden Rings Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Olympic Golden Rings Awards")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Rio_2016_%2828430991164%29.jpg/250px-Rio_2016_%2828430991164%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_2016_(28430991164).jpg)

[Olympic rings](/wiki/Olympic_rings "Olympic rings") displayed in Rio de Janeiro

In November 2017, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") announced the winners of the Golden rings in six categories for the best broadcast coverage of the Games. The Best Olympic Sports Production was awarded to Beach Volleyball, produced by Geoff Johnson and directed by Greg Breakell and Gary Milkis. The production for the cycling road race and Sailing came second and third. The next category was the best Olympic feature, for which [TV Globo](/wiki/TV_Globo "TV Globo")'s show *Esporte Espetacular* finished third, and [CCTV China](/wiki/CCTV_China "CCTV China")'s feature *A Sequel of Love* came second. The winner was [NBC Olympics](/wiki/NBC_Olympics "NBC Olympics") for their feature *The Most Beautiful Thing*. The third category was The Best Athlete Profile, for which [RTBF](/wiki/RTBF "RTBF") from [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") collected the third place prize for their profile of [Nafi Thiam](/wiki/Nafi_Thiam "Nafi Thiam"). Globo went one better than the previous category coming second with their profile of [Isaquias Queiroz](/wiki/Isaquias_Queiroz "Isaquias Queiroz"). The winner of the category again was NBC, this time for their piece on [Wayde van Niekerk](/wiki/Wayde_van_Niekerk "Wayde van Niekerk"). The Best On-Air Promotion was announced next, with the [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport") winning with NBC coming second this time and [BNT from Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgarian_National_Television "Bulgarian National Television") finishing third. The Best Olympic Digital Service went to NBC, with [ZDF](/wiki/ZDF "ZDF") and [SporTV](/wiki/SporTV "SporTV")/[Globosat](/wiki/Globosat "Globosat") picking up the second and third places. The Best Olympic Programme was awarded to SporTV/Globosat, while [TV Globo](/wiki/TV_Globo "TV Globo") and [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport") completed the podium.[[89]](#cite_note-94)

### Mascots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Mascots")]

Main article: [Vinicius and Tom](/wiki/Vinicius_and_Tom "Vinicius and Tom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/6/62/Vinicius_and_Tom.svg/250px-Vinicius_and_Tom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Vinicius_and_Tom.svg)

[Vinicius and Tom](/wiki/Vinicius_and_Tom "Vinicius and Tom"), the mascots of the 2016 Summer Olympics and [2016 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Paralympics "2016 Summer Paralympics"), respectively

On 24 November 2014, the [official mascots](/wiki/List_of_Olympic_mascots "List of Olympic mascots") of the 2016 Summer Olympics and Paralympics were unveiled, created by [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo")-based animation company Birdo.[[90]](#cite_note-MascotNames-95) The Olympic mascot [Vinicius](/wiki/Vinicius_and_Tom "Vinicius and Tom"), named after musician [Vinicius de Moraes](/wiki/Vinicius_de_Moraes "Vinicius de Moraes"), represents Brazilian wildlife and carries design traits of cats, monkeys, and birds.[[90]](#cite_note-MascotNames-95) According to their fictional backgrounds, the mascots "were both born from the joy of Brazilians after it was announced that Rio would host the Games".[[91]](#cite_note-96)

Brand director Beth Lula stated that the mascots were intended to reflect the diversity of Brazil's culture and people.[[92]](#cite_note-bbc-riomascot-97) The names of the mascots were determined by a public vote whose results were announced on 14 December 2014. The names, which reference the co-writers of the song "[The Girl from Ipanema](/wiki/The_Girl_from_Ipanema "The Girl from Ipanema")", won over two other sets of names, tallying 44 percent of 323,327 votes.[[93]](#cite_note-98) At the [Olympic wrestling events](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Wrestling at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), coaches were given plush dolls of Vinicius to throw into the ring when they wished to challenge a referee's call.[[94]](#cite_note-Gordon-99)

### Emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Emblem")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Parque_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Rio_2016_%2828370259403%29.jpg/250px-Parque_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Rio_2016_%2828370259403%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parque_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Rio_2016_(28370259403).jpg)

Sculpture of the *Rio 2016* logo in [Barra Olympic Park](/wiki/Barra_Olympic_Park "Barra Olympic Park")

The official emblem for the 2016 Summer Olympics was designed by Brazilian agency Tatíl Design and was unveiled on 31 December 2010, winning in a competition against 139 agencies.[[95]](#cite_note-adweek-100) The emblem represents three figures joined at their arms and feet, with the overall shape reflecting that of [Sugarloaf Mountain](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mountain_(Brazil) "Sugarloaf Mountain (Brazil)"). It was also designed to have a three-dimensional form, which designer Fred Gelli claimed made it the first 3D logo in the history of the Olympics.[[96]](#cite_note-dezeen-16emblem-101)

The logo has been noted as evoking [Henri Matisse](/wiki/Henri_Matisse "Henri Matisse")'s painting *[Dance](/wiki/Dance_(Matisse) "Dance (Matisse)")*. There were also allegations by the Colorado-based Telluride Foundation that the logo had been plagiarized from its own; while also consisting of several figures linked in motion, the Telluride Foundation logo contains four figures. This was not the first time that the foundation had alleged plagiarism of its logo by a Brazilian event; in 2004, the linked figures element had been copied for the logo of [Carnival](/wiki/Brazilian_Carnival "Brazilian Carnival") celebrations in [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia"). Gelli defended the allegations, stating that the concept of figures linked in embrace was not inherently original, as it was "an ancient reference" and "in the [collective unconscious](/wiki/Collective_unconscious "Collective unconscious")". Gelli cited *Dance* as an influence of the logo's concept and stated that the designers had intentionally aimed to make the interpretation of the concept as dissimilar to others as possible.[[97]](#cite_note-denverpost-telluride-102)

## Concerns and controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Concerns and controversies")]

Main article: [Concerns and controversies at the 2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Concerns_and_controversies_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Concerns and controversies at the 2016 Summer Olympics")

The lead-up to the Rio Games was marked by several controversies, including: Brazil's [political](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Dilma_Rousseff "Impeachment of Dilma Rousseff") and [economic crisis](/wiki/2014_Brazilian_economic_crisis "2014 Brazilian economic crisis"); the [Zika virus epidemic](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Zika_virus_epidemic "2015–16 Zika virus epidemic"); the significant [pollution in the Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay#Environment "Guanabara Bay"); and an ongoing [doping scandal](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia") involving [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Russia at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), which affected the participation of Russian athletes in the Games. However, the Zika virus was not contracted by anyone competing in or attending the Olympics,[[98]](#cite_note-103) and the Games went ahead normally with no major incident.[[99]](#cite_note-104)[[100]](#cite_note-105)[[101]](#cite_note-106)

### Political and economic crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Political and economic crisis")]

Main articles: [2014 Brazilian economic crisis](/wiki/2014_Brazilian_economic_crisis "2014 Brazilian economic crisis"), [Operation Car Wash](/wiki/Operation_Car_Wash "Operation Car Wash"), and [Impeachment of Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Dilma_Rousseff "Impeachment of Dilma Rousseff")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/01062016-_89A3476-Editar_%2827244602030%29.jpg/250px-01062016-_89A3476-Editar_%2827244602030%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:01062016-_89A3476-Editar_(27244602030).jpg)

Suspended president [Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Dilma_Rousseff "Dilma Rousseff") during an interview with [Al Jazeera](/wiki/Al_Jazeera_English "Al Jazeera English") at the [Alvorada Palace](/wiki/Alvorada_Palace "Alvorada Palace") on 1 June 2016

There is no legally legitimate evidence of impropriety in the choice of Rio to host the Olympic games, at least as far as the Brazilian Olympic Committee or the politicians involved in the process are concerned.[[102]](#cite_note-107) However, the specialized media raised suspicions about it. In 2014, [Operation Car Wash](/wiki/Operation_Car_Wash "Operation Car Wash"), an investigation by the [Federal Police of Brazil](/wiki/Federal_Police_of_Brazil "Federal Police of Brazil"), uncovered unprecedented [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") and corruption at the state-controlled oil company [Petrobras](/wiki/Petrobras "Petrobras"). In early 2015, a series of protests against alleged corruption by the government of President [Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Dilma_Rousseff "Dilma Rousseff") began in Brazil, triggered by revelations that numerous politicians were involved in the Petrobras affair. By early 2016, the scandal had escalated into a full-blown political crisis affecting not only President Rousseff, but also former President [Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva](/wiki/Luiz_In%C3%A1cio_Lula_da_Silva "Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva"), resulting in [widespread demonstrations involving millions of protesters](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_protests_in_Brazil "2015–16 protests in Brazil"),[[103]](#cite_note-108) both anti- and pro-Rousseff.[[104]](#cite_note-109)[[105]](#cite_note-110) At the same time, Brazil faced its worst economic recession since the 1990s, raising questions about whether the country was adequately prepared to host the Olympic Games against a volatile political and economic backdrop. On 12 May 2016, President Rousseff was stripped of her powers and duties for 180 days after an [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_process_against_Dilma_Rousseff "Impeachment process against Dilma Rousseff") vote in the [Federal Senate](/wiki/Brazilian_Senate "Brazilian Senate"), with Vice President [Michel Temer](/wiki/Michel_Temer "Michel Temer") standing in as [acting president](/wiki/Acting_president "Acting president") during the Games.[[106]](#cite_note-111)

On 5 October 2017, Brazilian Olympic Committee head [Carlos Nuzman](/wiki/Carlos_Nuzman "Carlos Nuzman") was arrested amid a money-laundering investigation into a $2 million payment that was allegedly made to secure votes for the bid to bring the Olympics to Rio. The money was believed to have been paid to former [IAAF](/wiki/IAAF "IAAF") president [Lamine Diack](/wiki/Lamine_Diack "Lamine Diack") and his son Papa Massata Diack, who was a member of the IOC at the time of the alleged payment, which was three days before the vote in 2009. All three were charged with money laundering, along with the former Rio state governor [Sérgio Cabral Filho](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Cabral_Filho "Sérgio Cabral Filho") (who was already in prison for money laundering offenses at the time), Brazilian businessman Arthur Soares, and ex-Brazilian Olympic Committee chief Leonardo Gryner. All six were charged with running a criminal organization, money laundering, and violating currency laws in their own native countries.[[107]](#cite_note-112) On 4 July 2019, it was reported that Cabral told a judge that the money paid to Diack was used to buy as many as nine votes.[[108]](#cite_note-113) Rio mayor [Eduardo Paes](/wiki/Eduardo_Paes "Eduardo Paes") was also accused of corruption and fraud in relation to the construction of a number of venues for the Games.[[109]](#cite_note-114)[[110]](#cite_note-115) In 2024, Nuzman's and Cabral's conviction in this legal case was overturned by the Court.[[111]](#cite_note-116)[[112]](#cite_note-117)

### Zika virus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Zika virus")]

Main article: [2015–16 Zika virus epidemic](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Zika_virus_epidemic "2015–16 Zika virus epidemic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Dengue_Votuporanga.jpg/250px-Dengue_Votuporanga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dengue_Votuporanga.jpg)

Agent for endemic diseases of the city of [Votuporanga](/wiki/Votuporanga "Votuporanga"), São Paulo

An [outbreak](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Zika_virus_epidemic "2015–16 Zika virus epidemic") of the mosquito-borne [Zika virus](/wiki/Zika_virus "Zika virus") in Brazil raised fears regarding its potential impact on athletes and visitors. To prevent puddles of stagnant water that allow mosquitoes to breed, organizers announced plans to perform daily inspections of Olympic venues.[[113]](#cite_note-118) Zika virus transmission was also attributed to [inefficient sewage treatment](#Sanitation) in the area, an issue that was also in the process of being addressed for the Games.[[114]](#cite_note-atlantic_20160331-119)

In May 2016, a group of 150 physicians and scientists sent an open letter to the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO), calling upon them to, according to co-author [Arthur Caplan](/wiki/Arthur_Caplan "Arthur Caplan"), have "an open, transparent discussion of the risks of holding the Olympics as planned in Brazil". The WHO dismissed the request, stating that "cancelling or changing the location of the 2016 Olympics [would] not significantly alter the international spread of Zika virus", and that there was "no public health justification" for postponing them.[[115]](#cite_note-quartz-goahead-120)[[116]](#cite_note-wp-150experts-121)[[117]](#cite_note-bbc-whono-122)

[Some athletes](/wiki/List_of_athletes_not_attending_Rio_Olympics_due_to_Zika_virus_concerns "List of athletes not attending Rio Olympics due to Zika virus concerns") did not attend the Games because of the epidemic.[[118]](#cite_note-123)[[119]](#cite_note-124) On 2 September 2016, however, the World Health Organization reported that there were no confirmed cases of Zika among athletes or visitors during the 2016 Olympics.[[120]](#cite_note-bbc-nozikaactually-125)

### Environmental problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Environmental problems")]

Main articles: [Environmental issues in Brazil](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Brazil "Environmental issues in Brazil") and [Water supply and sanitation in Brazil](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Brazil "Water supply and sanitation in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Forte_de_Copacabana_panorama.jpg/250px-Forte_de_Copacabana_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Forte_de_Copacabana_panorama.jpg)

[Fort Copacabana](/wiki/Fort_Copacabana "Fort Copacabana") hosted the [cycling road race](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Cycling at the 2016 Summer Olympics") (start and finish), [marathon swimming](/wiki/Marathon_swimming "Marathon swimming") and [triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon "Triathlon") events.

The [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay"), whose waters were used for sailing and windsurfing competitions, is heavily polluted. Among the chief causes of the pollution are uncollected trash fed into the bay via polluted rivers and [slums](/wiki/Slum "Slum") along the coast. Pollution of the Guanabara has been a long-term issue. At the [Earth Summit](/wiki/Earth_Summit "Earth Summit") in 1992, officials promised they would begin to address the pollution, but previous attempts to do so have been insufficient. As an aspect of their bid for the Games, Rio once again committed to making efforts towards cleaning the bay.[[121]](#cite_note-nyt-pollution-126)[[122]](#cite_note-127) However, some of these proposed initiatives have faced budgetary issues.[[33]](#cite_note-guardian-fundingclean-36) Prior to these efforts, only 17% of Rio's sewage was treated;[[123]](#cite_note-guardian-germannfect-128) this [raw sewage](/wiki/Raw_sewage "Raw sewage") also leaked into the bay. Although Rio mayor [Eduardo Paes](/wiki/Eduardo_Paes "Eduardo Paes") stated that the city might not be able to reach its goal of having 80% of sewage treated,[[124]](#cite_note-wp-superbug-129) at least 60% of sewage was treated by March 2016, with a projected goal of 65% of sewage being treated by the start of the Olympics.[[125]](#cite_note-130)

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Security")]

See also: [Crime in Brazil](/wiki/Crime_in_Brazil "Crime in Brazil") and [Terrorism in Brazil](/wiki/Terrorism_in_Brazil "Terrorism in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/For%C3%A7a_A%C3%A9rea_Brasileira_%28FAB%29_faz_treinamento_de_intercepta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_a%C3%A9rea_para_os_Jogos_Ol%C3%ADmpicos_de_2016.jpg/250px-For%C3%A7a_A%C3%A9rea_Brasileira_%28FAB%29_faz_treinamento_de_intercepta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_a%C3%A9rea_para_os_Jogos_Ol%C3%ADmpicos_de_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:For%C3%A7a_A%C3%A9rea_Brasileira_(FAB)_faz_treinamento_de_intercepta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_a%C3%A9rea_para_os_Jogos_Ol%C3%ADmpicos_de_2016.jpg)

[F-5EM Tiger II](/wiki/Northrop_F-5 "Northrop F-5") fighter jet of the [Brazilian Air Force](/wiki/Brazilian_Air_Force "Brazilian Air Force") during an air intercept training for Rio 2016

Rio's crime problems also received renewed attention after it was awarded the 2016 Games; mayor Paes stated that the city was facing "big issues" in heightening security, but that such concerns and issues were presented to the IOC throughout the bidding process.[[126]](#cite_note-131)

The governor of Rio de Janeiro also highlighted the fact that London faced security problems, with a [terrorist attack](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_bombings "7 July 2005 London bombings") occurring just a day after it was awarded the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"). The estimate was that 5,000 men of the [National Public Security Force](/wiki/National_Public_Security_Force "National Public Security Force") and 22,000 military officers (14,800 [Army](/wiki/Brazilian_Army "Brazilian Army"); 5,900 [Navy](/wiki/Brazilian_Navy "Brazilian Navy") and 1,300 of the [Brazilian Air Force](/wiki/Brazilian_Air_Force "Brazilian Air Force")), in addition to the fixed quota of Rio January, would act during the Olympic Games.[[127]](#cite_note-terror-132)

On 21 July 2016, two weeks before the scheduled start of the Games, the [Brazilian Federal Police](/wiki/Brazilian_Federal_Police "Brazilian Federal Police") broke up an [Islamic jihadist terrorist](/wiki/Islamic_terrorism "Islamic terrorism") cell named [Ansar al-Khilafah Brazil](/wiki/Ansar_al-Khilafah_Brazil "Ansar al-Khilafah Brazil") by arresting 12 people.[[128]](#cite_note-133)

### Russian doping scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Russian doping scandal")]

See also: [Doping in Russia](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia") and [McLaren Report](/wiki/McLaren_Report "McLaren Report")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/%D0%9E%D0%9A%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B8.JPG/250px-%D0%9E%D0%9A%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B8.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%9E%D0%9A%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B8.JPG)

Headquarters of the [Russian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee "Russian Olympic Committee") in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow")

In December 2014, media attention began growing when German broadcaster [ARD](/wiki/ARD_(broadcaster) "ARD (broadcaster)") reported on state-sponsored doping in Russia, comparing it to [doping in East Germany](/wiki/Doping_in_East_Germany "Doping in East Germany"). In November 2015, the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (WADA) published a report, and the [International Association of Athletics Federations](/wiki/International_Association_of_Athletics_Federations "International Association of Athletics Federations") (IAAF) suspended Russia indefinitely from world track and field events. The United Kingdom Anti-Doping agency later assisted WADA with testing in Russia. In June 2016, they reported they were unable to fully carry out their work, and noted intimidation by armed [Federal Security Service](/wiki/Federal_Security_Service "Federal Security Service") (FSB) agents.[[129]](#cite_note-134) After a Russian former lab director made allegations about the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") in [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), WADA commissioned an independent investigation led by [Richard McLaren](/wiki/Richard_McLaren_(academic) "Richard McLaren (academic)"), which found corroborating evidence, concluding in a report published in July 2016 that the [Ministry of Sport](/wiki/Ministry_of_Sport_(Russia) "Ministry of Sport (Russia)") and the FSB had operated a "state-directed failsafe system" using a "disappearing positive [test] methodology" (DPM) from "at least late 2011 to August 2015".[[130]](#cite_note-135)

In response to these findings, WADA announced that RUSADA should be regarded as non-compliant with respect to the World Anti-Doping Code, and recommended that Russia be banned from competing in the 2016 Summer Olympics.[[131]](#cite_note-136) The IOC rejected the recommendation, stating that the IOC and each sport's [international federation](/wiki/International_federation "International federation") would make decisions on each athlete's individual basis.[[132]](#cite_note-137)[[133]](#cite_note-138) A day before the opening ceremony, 278 athletes were cleared to compete under the Russian flag, while 111 were removed because of doping.[[134]](#cite_note-139) In contrast, the entire Kuwaiti team was banned from competing under their own flag for a non-doping related matter.[[135]](#cite_note-140)[[136]](#cite_note-141) Unlike the IOC, the [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") voted unanimously to ban the entire [Russian team](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2016_Summer_Paralympics "Russia at the 2016 Summer Paralympics") from the [2016 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Paralympics "2016 Summer Paralympics"), and suspended the [Russian Paralympic Committee](/wiki/Russian_Paralympic_Committee "Russian Paralympic Committee") after it found evidence that the DPM was also in operation at the [2014 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Paralympics "2014 Winter Paralympics").[[137]](#cite_note-142)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The official logo of the 2016 Summer Olympics was made in 3D design, made by Brazilian design company Tàtil Design; it was inspired by Brazil's rich history of festivals and its colorful people; it also symbolizes energy, passion and unity. The selected colours derived from the city's environment, symbolises the sun and Brazil's happy warm and nature.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Incumbent president [Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Dilma_Rousseff "Dilma Rousseff") suspended from duties due to [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Dilma_Rousseff "Impeachment of Dilma Rousseff").[[2]](#cite_note-3)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** The [Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese "Brazilian Portuguese") pronunciation is [[ˈʒɔɡuz oˈlĩpikuz dʒi veˈɾɐ̃w dʒi ˈdojz ˈmiw i dʒizeˈsejs]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese"), in Brazil's standard pronunciation.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-84)** Figures in table reflect all official changes in medal standings.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-89)** The [Independent Olympic Athletes](/wiki/Independent_Olympic_Athletes_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Independent Olympic Athletes at the 2016 Summer Olympics") team was composed of nine [Kuwaiti](/wiki/Kuwait_at_the_Olympics "Kuwait at the Olympics") athletes competing under the Olympic flag, as the [Kuwait Olympic Committee](/wiki/Kuwait_Olympic_Committee "Kuwait Olympic Committee") had been suspended by the IOC due to political interference.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-86)[[83]](#cite_note-87) While the [Refugee Olympic Team](/wiki/Refugee_Olympic_Team_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Refugee Olympic Team at the 2016 Summer Olympics") also competed under the Olympic flag, they were not considered independent.[[84]](#cite_note-88)
